news_summary: Recent discoveries reveal Bronze Age Britons innovatively repurposed ancient bones to create copper-mining tools, showcasing early resourcefulness and technological advancement.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes révèlent que les Britanniques de l'âge du bronze ont su faire preuve d'ingéniosité en réutilisant des os anciens pour fabriquer des outils destinés à l'exploitation du cuivre, ce qui témoigne de leur débrouillardise et de leurs avancées technologiques dès cette époque.
news_summary_es: Descubrimientos recientes revelan que los británicos de la Edad del Bronce reutilizaron de forma innovadora huesos antiguos para fabricar herramientas destinadas a la extracción de cobre, lo que pone de manifiesto su ingenio y sus avances tecnológicos ya en aquella época.

articles.title: Bronze Age Britons fashioned copper-mining tools out of old bones
articles.summary: An analysis of 150 artefacts from a site in Wales shows that the ancient practice of making tools out of bone persisted even after the advent of metal-working
articles.content:
An analysis of 150 artefacts from a site in Wales shows that the ancient practice of making tools out of bone persisted even after the advent of metal-working

Wedges made of limb bones may have been used for splitting soft, copper-bearing rock O. Zagorodnia

Even with the technology to make metal tools, people in Bronze Age Britain still used animal bone tools alongside metal ones to obtain copper, a practice spanning at least nine centuries between 3700 and 2800 years ago.

A study of 150 bones from the Bronze Age copper-mining complex at Great Orme in North Wales, UK, suggests the bones were deliberately chosen and shaped for specific mining tasks essential for copper extraction, especially in softer rock.

“It’s exciting because it challenges assumptions that Bronze Age mining was dominated by metal and stone tools; instead, we are seeing a more diverse and adaptable toolkit,” says Olga Zagorodnia at the British Museum, London, who conducted the work with Harriet White, an independent archaeologist.

Since the first archaeological excavations at the site in the early 1990s, over 30,000 bone fragments have been discovered. Previous studies focused on species identification, finding that over half belonged to cattle, with others mainly coming from sheep, goats and pigs. But it was suspected some bones were used as tools. A 2011 study identified wear related to use as a tool and technological features on a small number of them.

Now, Zagorodnia and White have taken a close-up look at the wear marks on 150 of the bones’ surfaces with high-resolution microscopy. They then performed experiments that mimicked mining activities with replica bone tools in order to compare the wear marks they produced with those on the ancient bones.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

“One of the most striking aspects was how quickly use-wear developed, which helped validate what we were seeing under the microscope,” says White. “When we carried out experiments in splitting bone leading to circular fracture patterns, we could observe those same fractures in the archaeological examples, which really connected us to the past people at the site.”

The results suggested that the bones had been deliberately fashioned into several distinct tool types. These included wedges made of limb bone, possibly used for splitting soft, copper-bearing rock. Intentional tapering and polish on certain bone sections suggested that the implements were attached to handles and used in a similar way to metal picks, a finding that has never been reported before.

Scoops made from scapula or pelvis bones may have been used for raking or handling loose ore O. Zagorodnia

Meanwhile, ribs were possibly used for scraping soft, sandy limestone. They might also have been used as stirrers or scrapers during processes that used water to separate and concentrate copper from the ore. The team also identified scapula or pelvis-based scoops, which may have been used to rake or shovel loose ore.

“What we may be seeing at Great Orme is not a new invention, but the continuation of a long-standing technological tradition [predating metal-working],” says Zagorodnia. Bone was a readily available material in pastoral societies and it was much less labour- and resource-intensive to make a tool from bone than from metal, according to the researchers.

The findings at Great Orme parallel other prehistoric mining sites in Europe where bone mining tools have also been found. “Communities in Bronze Age Britain and Europe were not simply replacing bone with metal materials; they were used alongside each other,” says White. “It suggests a deep practical understanding of material properties and a flexible approach to technology. It also points to organised mining practices, with specialised tools and likely skilled workers.”

“Both authors have brought a level of scientific discrimination not previously seen to this unique field of artefact study,” says Simon Timberlake, a freelance archaeologist based in Cambridge, UK. “The only way we can understand the people who helped change the technology of the Stone Age to the Metal Age is through the study of the tools they used.”

Journal reference: Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports DOI: 10.1016/j.jasrep.2026.105751
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery that enhances understanding of Bronze Age technology and mining practices, revealing a sophisticated and adaptable use of materials. This insight into ancient human innovation and cultural practices has broad educational and historical value, inspiring curiosity and appreciation for human ingenuity over time.
articles.personality_title: Bronze Age miners in Wales used bone tools alongside metal for copper mining
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Bronze Age Britain, around 3700 to 2800 years ago, people began working with metal, especially copper. Archaeologists have studied a mining site called Great Orme in North Wales, where copper was extracted during this time.

**What happened** – Researchers examined 150 animal bones found at Great Orme and discovered they had been shaped and used as tools for mining copper. These bone tools included wedges, scrapers, and scoops made from different bones like limb bones, ribs, and shoulder blades. The team compared wear marks on the ancient bones with those on modern replicas used in mining experiments to confirm their purpose.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that Bronze Age miners did not rely only on metal tools but also used bone tools for specific tasks. Using bone was easier and required fewer resources than making metal tools. This reveals that people at that time had a smart and flexible approach to technology, combining old and new methods for better results.

**What's next step** – Archaeologists will continue studying these bone tools and other mining sites to learn more about how ancient people worked and lived. This research could help us better understand early mining techniques and the skills of Bronze Age communities.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Bronze Age miners in Wales skillfully used bone tools alongside metal ones, showing a clever mix of old and new technologies to mine copper effectively.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les mineurs de l’âge du bronze au Pays de Galles utilisaient des outils en os avec le métal pour extraire le cuivre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Grande-Bretagne à l’âge du bronze, il y a environ 3700 à 2800 ans, les gens ont commencé à travailler le métal, notamment le cuivre. Les archéologues ont étudié un site minier appelé Great Orme, en pays de Galles du Nord, où le cuivre était extrait à cette époque.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont examiné 150 os d’animaux trouvés à Great Orme et ont découvert qu’ils avaient été façonnés et utilisés comme outils pour l’extraction du cuivre. Ces outils en os comprenaient des coins, des grattoirs et des pelles fabriqués à partir de différents os comme les os des membres, les côtes et les omoplates. L’équipe a comparé les traces d’usure sur les os anciens avec celles d’outils modernes utilisés pour des expériences minières afin de confirmer leur usage.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les mineurs de l’âge du bronze n’utilisaient pas seulement des outils en métal, mais aussi des outils en os pour des tâches spécifiques. L’os était plus facile à travailler et demandait moins de ressources que le métal. Cela révèle que les gens de cette époque avaient une approche intelligente et flexible de la technologie, combinant méthodes anciennes et nouvelles pour de meilleurs résultats.

**Prochaine étape** – Les archéologues poursuivront l’étude de ces outils en os et d’autres sites miniers pour en apprendre davantage sur le mode de vie et le travail des anciens. Ces recherches pourraient permettre de mieux comprendre les techniques minières anciennes et les compétences des communautés de l’âge du bronze.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les mineurs de l’âge du bronze au Pays de Galles utilisaient habilement des outils en os en plus des outils en métal, montrant un mélange astucieux d’anciennes et de nouvelles technologies pour extraire efficacement le cuivre.
articles.personality_title_es: Los mineros de la Edad de Bronce en Gales usaban herramientas de hueso junto al metal para extraer cobre
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la Gran Bretaña de la Edad de Bronce, hace entre 3700 y 2800 años, la gente empezó a trabajar con metales, especialmente el cobre. Los arqueólogos estudiaron un sitio minero llamado Great Orme, en el norte de Gales, donde se extraía cobre en esa época.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores examinaron 150 huesos de animales encontrados en Great Orme y descubrieron que fueron moldeados y usados como herramientas para extraer cobre. Estas herramientas de hueso incluían cuñas, raspadores y palas hechas de diferentes huesos como huesos de las extremidades, costillas y omóplatos. El equipo comparó las marcas de uso en los huesos antiguos con las de réplicas modernas usadas en experimentos mineros para confirmar su función.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que los mineros de la Edad de Bronce no usaban solo herramientas de metal, sino también herramientas de hueso para tareas específicas. El hueso era más fácil de trabajar y necesitaba menos recursos que el metal. Esto revela que la gente de esa época tenía un enfoque inteligente y flexible hacia la tecnología, combinando métodos antiguos y nuevos para obtener mejores resultados.

**Próximo paso** – Los arqueólogos continuarán estudiando estas herramientas de hueso y otros sitios mineros para aprender más sobre cómo trabajaban y vivían las personas antiguas. Esta investigación puede ayudar a entender mejor las técnicas mineras y las habilidades de las comunidades de la Edad de Bronce.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los mineros de la Edad de Bronce en Gales usaron herramientas de hueso junto con las de metal, mostrando una combinación inteligente de tecnologías antiguas y nuevas para extraer cobre de manera efectiva.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of ancient bone tools—limb bone wedges, scapula scoops, and rib scrapers—arranged carefully on a textured stone surface beside soft, copper-bearing rocks, with subtle impressions of mining activity suggested by fine dust and faint tool marks, rendered in natural earthy colors and soft light.
